_comment_biomass_scale: BIOMASS UNIT ANCHOR: ~15 lb per biomass unit. Validated by the large-animal body_mass values (mammoth 800 ≈ 12,000 lb, steppe_runner 120 ≈ 1,800 lb, aurochs 80 ≈ 1,200 lb). Keep new body_mass and carry/take levers (hunt.per_worker_biomass_capacity, expedition hunt.per_worker_carry) consistent with this scale — they encode a real physical human carry/processing capacity, so 'only ratios matter' does NOT apply to them.
_comment_species_regrowth_rate: PER-SPECIES WILD REGROWTH RATE r (Grazing Phase 2b-ii) — the logistic breeding rate a WILD herd of this species grows at, replacing the retired single global ecology.regrowth_rate (0.05) that bred every animal — rabbit and mammoth alike — at the same rate. That uniform rate was the artifact behind PR #117's 'small game can't provision an expedition': a rabbit warren's MSY (= r*K/4) was crippled by a megafauna's r. Now fast small game breeds hot and slow megafauna cold: rabbit/fowl 0.35, deer/boar 0.10, migratory (mammoth/steppe_runner/marsh_grazer) 0.04. Only WILD herds read this; the pastoral (0.25) and pen (0.90) husbandry rungs keep their own faster r (fauna::herd_ecology), and the phase bands stay shared. STARTING ANCHORS — measure and retune (docs/plan_grazing_2b.md §5). NOTE the ladder tension this creates for fast breeders: a wild rabbit's r=0.35 EXCEEDS the pastoral rung's 0.25, so taming a rabbit is a growth DOWNGRADE — flagged for the 2d pen/pastoral retune, not fixed here.
_comment_husbandry_ceiling: HOW FAR UP THE HUSBANDRY LADDER A SPECIES CLIMBS (Grazing 2d-delta, docs/plan_grazing_2d.md §4a): wild (hunt-only — domestication never accrues, tame/corral/extend_pen reject) < pastoral (tames + roams, never pens — corral/extend_pen reject) < pen (the full ladder). A single ceiling, not two flags, so 'pennable but not tameable' is unrepresentable. Defaults to 'pen' when omitted (the pre-delta universal behaviour). Roster: mammoth/deer/seal = wild (hunted, not herded — seal is a marine forager, a non-grazing constant-K haul-out colony), steppe_runner/marsh_grazer = pastoral (nomadic herding), boar/rabbit/fowl = pen (pigs/hutches/poultry).
_comment_taming_rate: PER-SPECIES TAMING TIMESCALE (intensification ladder slice 3c, docs/plan_intensification_ladder.md) — a multiplier on the animal:pastoral rung's BUILD, so the rung owns the mechanic and the species scales it (exactly how husbandry r works: rung pastoral_gain x species wild r). Without it the rung's single progress_per_turn (0.04) tames EVERY species in the same 25 turns — a rabbit costs what a Steppe Runner costs. Taming a small, quick, forgiving animal should be fast; binding a large migratory herd should be generational. IT SCALES THE WHOLE TIMESCALE: RungDef::build_accrual AND build_decay both take it, so the rung's 4:1 build:decay ratio is invariant — slow to tame, SLOW TO FORGET (a beast that takes a lifetime to gentle does not go feral in a season). Scaling progress alone would put a Steppe Runner at 0.04*0.2 = 0.008/turn, BELOW the 0.01/turn decay: literally untameable, and a violation of the ladder's taming-must-outrun-its-decay bound. Roster: rabbit/fowl/crag_goat 1.0 (25 turns), boar 0.8 (~31), aurochs 0.5 (50), steppe_runner/marsh_grazer 0.2 (125); deer/mammoth omit it — a wild husbandry_ceiling never tames at all. Defaults to 1.0 (the rung's own pace) when omitted. PLAYTEST DIALS — possibly slow, deliberately tunable.
_comment_body_mass: BIOMASS OF ONE ANIMAL (intensification ladder slice 8, docs/plan_intensification_ladder.md) — the quantum every hunt take is floored to. A herd's animal count is biomass/body_mass, DERIVED, never stored. This is what makes a herd a herd and not a fluid: you kill WHOLE animals, and a big animal is a lot of food at once. Read each row against its MSY (r*K/4) — the ratio IS the gameplay. (1) RHYTHM = body_mass/MSY turns per animal at the operating point: fowl/rabbit ~every turn (a trickle), boar/deer ~2, steppe_runner ~3, MAMMOTH ~7 — then you eat for a week. When the herd cannot yet spare a whole animal the hunt PAUSES and the herd regrows; that wait is constant escapement, discretised, and the herd's own biomass is the accumulator (there is no credit meter). (2) PARTY SIZE = HOW MUCH OF THE KILL YOU KEEP, against labor_config's hunt.per_worker_biomass_capacity (40): one hunter keeps 80% of a boar, 33% of a steppe runner, 5% of a mammoth; ~20 hunters bring a whole mammoth home. A party that cannot carry a whole animal may still take one and WASTE most of it — you cannot half-kill a mammoth, and that waste is the player's call, shown on the income breakdown. NOTE THE ASYMMETRY WITH PLANTS, IT IS DELIBERATE: forage never quantises, because you harvest grain by the handful. The PEN never quantises either (fauna::managed_yield_biomass) — converting a lumpy wild resource into a steady supply is WHY humans penned animals. PLAYTEST DIALS.
_comment_animals_per_herder: STANDING HERDER LABOR, PER SPECIES (intensification ladder slice 8): how many ANIMALS one herder can mind. herders_needed = ceil((biomass / body_mass) / animals_per_herder), owed EVERY TURN by a pastoral or penned herd — including wait turns when the herd cannot spare an animal. Just because you aren't killing an animal doesn't mean you aren't tending them, keeping them from running off, repairing fences. Before this a pen of 2 and a pen of 200 needed the same single keeper and only the FEED scaled. HERDING IS HEADS, NOT TONNES — the denominator is the whole design: a shepherd minds ~300 sheep, a cowherd ~80 cattle; you watch INDIVIDUALS (chase strays, check each animal) and a heavier beast is not proportionally more work. An earlier cut used a single global biomass_per_herder, which says 'one herder per 100 fowl but one herder per 2 boar' and invented a 45-herder steppe megaherd that was a pure artifact of the unit (4,560 biomass of Steppe Runner is only 38 ANIMALS = ~3 herders). Per-species for the same reason body_mass / taming_rate / husbandry_ceiling are. ONE NEED, NOT TWO: the same herders mind the herd AND slaughter it, so a managed rung has no separate harvest-side worker need. A WILD hunt is the opposite model and must stay apart — no maintenance (the herd isn't yours) but it keeps its carry cap: hunt = reach + carry, harvest = maintain + take. UNDERSTAFFING IS PROPORTIONAL, NEVER BINARY: herded_fraction = min(1, assigned/needed) scales growth and decays the rung's meter, floored and recoverable (the pen_fed_fraction -> starve_shrink_rate pattern). Binary escape survives ONLY for total abandonment (zero herders) — a threshold that destroys a 25-turn investment on rounding, as biomass breathes across it, is not a decision but an accident. Roster: fowl/rabbit 200 (birds and hutches — tiny, docile, one keeper minds a whole flock), crag_goat 80 (small hardy stock, herded in big flocks), boar 15, steppe_runner/marsh_grazer 15 (bison / wild-horse scale, herded not fenced), aurochs 12 (cattle — big, ornery, few per herder). deer/mammoth omit it: a wild husbandry_ceiling is never herded. Defaults to 25 when omitted. Resolved LIVE by display name (FaunaConfig::animals_per_herder_for), not cached on the Herd, so a retune reaches herds already on the map. PLAYTEST DIALS — ship these, measure, then tune.
_comment_husbandry_density: PER-SPECIES HUSBANDRY DENSITY (K) MULTIPLIER, PER RUNG — domestication makes the LAND hold more animals, and by how much is species-specific and NON-LINEAR up the ladder (wild x1, pastoral modest, pen big). pastoral_density scales a mobile-tamed herd's carrying capacity, pen_density a penned herd's; both default to 1.0 (neutral — a wild/untagged species is unchanged and byte-identical). This is DISTINCT from the global r-gains (husbandry.pastoral_gain 2.0 / pen_gain 4.0): those scale a herd's breeding RATE, this scales its CEILING. Without it a species on marginal range (a goat penned at K≈24) stays tiny even penned, and a fast wild breeder (rabbit) out-yields the prime domesticates (goat/aurochs) at every rung — because taming touched only r. Applied at the ONE K seam (fauna::ecological_carrying_capacity, via herd_density_gain) to the final range/footprint-derived K, resolved LIVE by display name so a retune reaches herds already on the map. Roster: crag_goat/aurochs 2.0/5.0 (the prime grazer domesticates — a fenced pasture packs far more of them), boar 1.5/4.0 (a tier-1 domesticate — a dense sty, just under the grazers since pigs lean harder on larder feed than rangeland stock), rabbit/fowl 1.1/1.5 (already dense as wild small game — little headroom), steppe_runner/marsh_grazer 1.5/1.0 (nomadic pastoral only — never penned, so pen_density is inert). deer/mammoth omit both (wild ceiling — never domesticated, always x1). PLAYTEST DIALS. Validated finite & >= 1.0 (a gain below 1 would make domestication REDUCE capacity).
_comment_hunt_policies: THE FOUR HUNT POLICIES ARE ASCENDING MULTIPLES OF MSY (slice 8b, fauna::hunt_policy_ceiling). Sustain = min(MSY, escapement above K/2) — the sustainable yield, CAPPED so a full herd declines GENTLY at MSY/turn and settles at K/2 (no one-turn burst); takes 0 below K/2 so a depleted herd recovers. Surplus = surplus_multiplier * MSY (1.5). Market = market_multiplier * MSY (2.5). Eradicate = everything. ORDERING IS GUARANTEED BY CONSTRUCTION: they are multiples of the SAME base, so Sustain <= 1x < 1.5x < 2.5x <= B at every biomass and every species — 'each option takes more than the previous', the property that failed under the retired independent skims (a fraction of B could dip below MSY for a fast breeder: measured Wild Fowl Sustain 0.22 vs a 0.10*B Surplus 0.15, inverted). EXTINCTION IS REAL AND ON-MAP: constant catch above MSY has no equilibrium, so Surplus slowly declines a herd (reversible if switched back) and Market drives it extinct. WHY A MULTIPLE OF MSY IS SAFE WHERE A multiplier*MSY FLOW WAS NOT: a flow was the whole take (constant in B, so floor(ceiling/body_mass) that is 0 on turn 1 is 0 forever — 7 of 9 species unhuntable); here MSY sizes only the RATE and the take is still clamped to the standing biomass, so a big animal on a small herd becomes huntable once the herd has grown one body. RETIRED and STAYS retired: follow.surplus_multiplier, surplus.take_fraction, market.take_fraction, hunt.take_fraction/min_take (Eradicate takes everything, no dial). Validate pins 1 < surplus_multiplier < market_multiplier. PLAYTEST DIALS.

hunt._comment_surplus_escapement_fraction: EXPEDITION-ONLY (systems::expeditions). A greedy hunting RAID grabs the herd's standing surplus down to a per-policy floor and comes home: Sustain 0.50*K > Surplus surplus_escapement_fraction*K (0.30) > Market ecology.collapse_fraction*K (0.15) > Eradicate 0. So more hunters take more animals in fewer-or-equal turns, and a deeper policy leaves a leaner herd. This does NOT touch the RESIDENT band's kill-credit take (still surplus_multiplier*MSY). Validate pins collapse_fraction < surplus_escapement_fraction < 0.5 (MSY_BIOMASS_FRACTION). PLAYTEST DIAL.

ecology._comment_collapse_fraction: The Allee/depensation threshold: below collapse_fraction * K the group declines irreversibly (collapse_rate/turn) even if hunting stops — the overhunting point of no return that turns Surplus/Market's steady overdraw into extinction. (It briefly doubled as Market's escapement floor in slice 8's ordered-floors cut; slice 8b made the hunt policies multiples of MSY, so this is once again ONLY the depensation threshold.)

husbandry._comment_ladder: THE HUSBANDRY YIELD LADDER IS FLOW-BASED: every rung pays MSY (= r*K/4 * hunt.provisions_per_biomass), and the rungs differ in the r that MSY is computed against — management buys a GROWTH RATE, not a licence to eat the standing stock. Since Grazing 2d the managed r is PER-SPECIES: pastoral r = min(husbandry_regrowth_cap, wild_r * pastoral_gain), pen r = min(husbandry_regrowth_cap, wild_r * pen_gain) — a penned rabbit (r cap-bound at 1.0, booms) and a penned mammoth (r 0.16, a long-haul investment) are different economies, retiring the retired flat 0.25/0.90. A penned herd's K is its FENCED FOOTPRINT's graze flow (capacity_fraction is retired), so it grazes its own land and the larder only pays what the pasture can't cover. Every managed harvest DRAWS THE HERD DOWN: the herd converges on K/2 and pays r*K/4 forever. See docs/plan_grazing_2d.md and docs/plan_corral_managed_population.md.
husbandry._comment_gains: PER-SPECIES HUSBANDRY GROWTH (Grazing 2d §3): the managed rungs scale each herd's OWN wild r rather than flattening every animal to one rate. pastoral_gain 2.0 (mobile-tamed grows 2x wild) < pen_gain 4.0 (penned grows 4x wild — protected, fed, bred), both clamped to husbandry_regrowth_cap 1.0 so a fast breeder stays in the stable logistic band (no overshoot/oscillation). RETUNE (intensification-ladder payoff): pastoral_gain 1.5 -> 2.0 and pen_gain 3.0 -> 4.0 lift the Tame/Corral rungs clearly above the fauna surplus_multiplier (1.5), so a managed rung out-yields the extractive Surplus option instead of coinciding with it; the cap 0.75 -> 1.0 stops squashing fast breeders' pen rate. Playtest levers — expect to tune (like the consumption dial).
husbandry._comment_pen_radius_max: PEN EXTENSION CAP (Grazing 2d-beta, the ExtendPen command): the largest fenced-footprint radius a pen may reach. 2 -> up to a 19-tile footprint (hex disk 1, 7, 19); each ring is a 25-turn keeper-labor investment (corral_build_progress_per_turn). Raise to let pens grow into larger self-feeding operations at more forgone-yield turns.
husbandry._comment_herders_hysteresis_fraction: HERDER-REQUIREMENT HYSTERESIS DEADBAND, as a fraction of animals_per_herder. herders_needed = ceil(animals / animals_per_herder) flickers +/-1 every turn when a Sustain-hunted slow herd's biomass breathes across an animals_per_herder multiple (the lumpy whole-animal kill) -- e.g. a Wild Aurochs near 12 head flips 1<->2, and since the herded_fraction decay lags a turn behind, the player is told 'staff all 1' then 'staff all 2' and can't satisfy either, slipping the tameness. Herd::stabilize_herders_needed (run every turn in advance_husbandry) breaks the flicker asymmetrically: UP IMMEDIATELY when the raw need rises (under-herding is harmful), DOWN only once the herd falls below (current-1)*animals_per_herder - animals_per_herder*this (a genuine multi-band drop, not a one-animal dip). 0.25 ~ a quarter of a herder's flock, enough to absorb the +/-1 oscillation; 0 disables the deadband (raw stateless flicker). PLAYTEST DIAL.

pastoral._comment: The MOBILE DOMESTICATED rung: passive (no worker, no upkeep — a roaming herd grazes for free). Its growth is now PER-SPECIES (wild_r * pastoral_gain, capped), so a tamed herd grows faster than a wild one but by a MULTIPLE of its own rate. The ecology block below carries only the shared phase bands; its regrowth_rate is unused (folded per-species by fauna::herd_ecology).

pen._comment: The PENNED rung: the ladder's top growth rate (pen_gain 4x wild, capped) — and the only rung with a running cost, because a confined herd must be FED whatever its fenced footprint can't graze. Its K is the footprint's graze flow / fodder (recomputed each turn — penned herds are no longer frozen), so a pen on lush steppe feeds itself for free and a pen on scrub drains the granary. The ecology block carries only phase bands; regrowth_rate is unused (per-species).
pen._comment_upkeep_per_biomass: FEED — UNCHANGED, deliberately: the running cost is the point of the arc. Food/turn drawn from the keeper's larder per unit of standing biomass — but only for the share the FOOTPRINT cannot graze: larder_upkeep = upkeep_per_biomass * biomass * (1 - pasture_fraction), where pasture_fraction = footprint_intake / (fodder_per_biomass * biomass). BEST-CASE net-positive floor (Grazing 2d §2.4, was an every-pen guarantee): FaunaConfig::validate() enforces only that the FASTEST breeder's pen stays net-positive even when fully larder-fed — u < r_pen*p/(2+r_pen) for r_pen = min(cap, max_wild_r * pen_gain) = min(1.0, 0.35*4.0) = 1.0, so the bound is 1.0*0.02/3.0 = 0.0067; shipped 0.002 sits inside it. A slow breeder or a poor-pasture pen may run at a LOSS BY DESIGN (bad placement, not a config error).
pen._comment_starve_shrink_rate: STARVATION: an underfed pen shrinks by starve_shrink_rate * (1 - fed_fraction) * biomass each turn, floored at ecology.extinction_floor * K_pen — the herd withers to a remnant and RECOVERS when fed again (it never despawns and never loses the pen). 0.10 = a fully-unfed herd loses 10%/turn: slow enough that a bad winter is survivable and visibly recoverable, fast enough that sustained neglect really does reduce the pen to a remnant.

husbandry._comment_corral_build: The pen's BUILD dials (the old corralling_yield_fraction 0.50 and corral_build_progress_per_turn 0.04) live in intensification_ladder.json -> the animal:pen rung's `build` block: both food webs climb on the same numbers. What remains here is the animal web's own economy.
husbandry._comment_knowledge: The earned-knowledge dials (the old knowledge_progress_per_turn 0.05 / knowledge_completion_threshold 1.0) moved to intensification_ladder.json -> the ladder's `knowledge` block in slice 4, at the same values. They were duplicated VERBATIM in labor_config's forage.cultivation, back when each web had its own hard-coded earn site; the earn path is one rung-driven seam now (RungDef::knowledge_earned), so the pace of a lesson is the LADDER's number, not the animal web's.

_comment_market: MARKET keeps only its TRADE RATE here. Its take is hunt.market_multiplier * MSY (2.5x — the commercial cull that drives a herd extinct); take_fraction is retired. See _comment_hunt_policies.
_comment_graze: THE GRAZE (PASTURE) LAYER — docs/plan_grazing_foundation.md. The land's ANIMAL-edible vegetal stock (grass, browse, forbs), on ANY vegetated land tile. It is a DIFFERENT stock on a DIFFERENT distribution from the human-edible forage (labor_config.json `forage`, seeds/nuts/tubers, food-module tiles only): humans cannot digest cellulose, and that is the entire economic basis of herding. Phase 2a ships the layer INERT — it regrows, persists and exports, and NOTHING reads it for gameplay yet.

graze._comment_capacity_by_biome: A DATA TABLE OVER THE 38 BIOMES, NOT A FORMULA. Grazeable biomass (grass, browse, forbs — cellulose humans cannot digest) a tile of each biome carries at capacity. Every biome must appear (validate() rejects a partial table — a missing row would silently read as an invisible zero-graze dead zone). READ IT AGAINST ITS TWIN: labor_config.json's forage.capacity_by_biome is the same 38 rows for the HUMAN food web, and the two are meant to DISAGREE — that disagreement is the agropastoral decision (docs/plan_grazing_foundation.md §1). Anchor: PrairieSteppe = 240 is THE reference pasture (and only 70 forage: grass feeds animals, humans get seed heads); every other row is a claim about that biome relative to it. The ABSOLUTE scale is a free parameter — only the ratios matter until Phase 2b's fodder_per_biomass denominates it into animals. THE INVERSIONS: MixedWoodland (55) / BorealTaiga (40) are poor pasture and rich forage (190/90) — a closed canopy shades out the ground cover but drops nuts and mast. The silt lowlands (AlluvialPlain 140, Floodplain/RiverDelta 130 — LOWERED from 230/230/220, where they were tied with prairie for best pasture) are THE FARM, NOT THE PASTURE: their value moved to the human web (195-210), where a river valley's silt and water actually belong. Alluvial is additionally the tag solver's fallback biome, so leaving it tied for best pasture also baked a worldgen artifact into the fauna model.
graze._comment_navigable_river: The NavigableRiver graze row (0) is VESTIGIAL — a navigable hex now grazes its UNDERLYING biome (resource_terrain), so a navigable-over-grassland hex grazes like grassland and this row is bypassed. Left only to keep the table total. (No river bonus on graze — you don't pasture animals on the channel.)
graze._comment_ecology: GRASS REGROWS FAST — it is the quickest-renewing vegetal stock in the model, and that is the economic premise of herding: a pasture eaten to the ground is back within a few seasons, a nut grove is not. Ordering (a claim about biology, not a knob): wild fauna 0.05 << forage 0.25 (labor_config.json) < GRAZE 0.40 << a fed pen 0.90 (a hyper-managed system, not a wild one). At r = 0.40 a tile's sustainable flow is r*K/4 = 0.10*K per turn and a stripped pasture climbs back to ~90% of capacity in ~20 turns (vs ~35 at forage's 0.25). collapse_rate is INERT for graze — grass has no Allee depensation, so advance_graze_regrowth runs pure logistic regrowth and never the collapse branch; the shared EcologyConfig simply carries the field (exactly as labor_config's forage ecology does). collapse_fraction/stressed_fraction are read, for the phase bands the overgrazing readout uses.
graze._comment_reseed_floor_fraction: The exact mirror of forage.reseed_floor_fraction. Grass reseeds from the surrounding ground, so GRAZE IS NEVER PERMANENTLY DEAD: an eaten-out tile is lifted to this small standing crop before regrowth each turn and recovers via the normal logistic curve, instead of sticking at 0 forever (logistic_regrowth(0, ..) == 0). Kept BELOW collapse_fraction (validated) so a stripped pasture still reads Collapsing — the floor stops permanent death, it does not hide overgrazing.
graze._comment_overgraze_escapement_fraction: THE OVERGRAZING ESCAPEMENT FLOOR (Grazing 2b-ii). Grazing (advance_herd_grazing) may draw a patch down to this fraction of capacity but NO LOWER in a turn. This is the constant-ESCAPEMENT discipline that makes the coupled herd<->graze system converge (docs/plan_grazing_2b.md §2.2 — the same lesson the corral learned when constant-catch MSY was unstable). WHY: the herd's demand (fodder*B) is a constant-CATCH draw on the graze, and a catch that strips a patch past the point where its regrowth can refill the offtake collapses the range into a permanently-stripped attractor at the reseed floor — the herd surviving as a stunted remnant on dead ground (MEASURED: at the bare 0.02 reseed floor an overgrazed rabbit range locks at graze 2.8% / herd 11% of K; at this 0.25 floor it recovers to graze ~38% / herd ~95% of K). Bounds K below at graze_sustainable_flow(0.25*cap)/fodder ≈ 0.84*K_max, so overgrazing can shrink a herd by at most ~16% — a MODEST but stable force; LOWER this for more dramatic overgrazing at rising crash risk. Validated: > reseed_floor_fraction (a real escapement, not just the reseed lift) and < 0.5 (the graze MSY point — overgrazing below the most-productive intensity stays possible and visible). STARTING ANCHOR — measure and retune (grazing_2b_convergence.rs).
